questions.question_text: An Idaho real estate trust account must be in whose name?
questions.explanation: Trust accounts must be in the broker's licensed business name and identified as a 'real estate trust account.' This ensures accountability and allows Commission audit and oversight.

questions.question_text: What type of account must a broker use for entrusted real estate funds?
questions.correct_answer: An account subject to withdrawal on demand
distractors: A money market account with restrictions
A savings account with penalty for early withdrawal
questions.explanation: Trust account funds must be subject to withdrawal on demand. This ensures funds are immediately available when needed for transactions. Restricted or time-locked accounts are not permitted.

questions.question_text: How often must a broker prepare accounting records for the trust account?
questions.explanation: Idaho requires separate monthly accounting records for trust accounts, including deposits, disbursements, and balances. Monthly reconciliation helps detect errors or unauthorized activity promptly.

questions.question_text: A broker who does not handle entrusted funds in any transaction must do what?
questions.correct_answer: File a Trust Account Notification form with IREC
distractors: Report it on their annual tax return
questions.explanation: Even if a broker does not receive entrusted funds, they must file a Trust Account Notification form with the Commission. This ensures IREC knows whether each brokerage handles trust funds.

questions.question_text: Trust account records must include which of the following?
distractors: Only deposits and the current balance
questions.explanation: Complete trust account records require documentation of all deposits, all disbursements, and running balances. This allows for proper reconciliation and Commission audit.

questions.question_text: By when must an Idaho broker deposit earnest money into the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: The next banking day after receipt
questions.explanation: Section 54-2045 requires deposit on or before the banking day immediately following receipt of funds. If received on Friday, the deposit must be made by the next banking day (typically Monday).

questions.question_text: A buyer gives earnest money to a salesperson on Saturday. By when must it be deposited?
questions.explanation: The deposit deadline is the banking day immediately following receipt. Since Saturday is not typically a banking day, and Sunday is not, the deposit must be made on Monday (the next banking day).

questions.question_text: Under what circumstance can a broker delay depositing earnest money beyond the next banking day?
questions.correct_answer: When written instructions from interested parties direct otherwise
distractors: When the broker is on vacation
When waiting for the contract to be fully executed
questions.explanation: The only exception to the next-banking-day deposit rule is when written instructions signed by the parties having an interest in the funds direct the broker to handle them differently.

questions.question_text: The parties in a transaction provide written instructions for the earnest money to be delivered to a title company. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, parties may direct transfer to a third party in writing
distractors: No, funds must always go to the broker's trust account
Only if the title company is IREC-approved
questions.explanation: Parties may direct the broker in writing to transfer funds to a third party such as a title or escrow company. This is a recognized alternative to depositing in the broker's trust account.

questions.question_text: Which Idaho Code section governs trust account deposit requirements?
questions.explanation: Section 54-2045 establishes the deposit requirements for entrusted funds, including the next-banking-day deadline. Section 54-2053 covers advertising, 54-2055 covers licensee property disclosure, and 54-2082 begins agency law.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller dispute who should receive the earnest money after a failed transaction. What governs the broker's handling?
questions.explanation: Commission Guideline 14, together with Idaho Code 54-2047, governs disputed earnest money: the broker notifies each party in writing of the other's demand and holds the funds until the parties resolve the dispute or a court orders disbursement.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits personal funds into the real estate trust account. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling of personal and trust funds
questions.explanation: Mixing personal funds with trust funds is commingling, which is prohibited. The only exception: the broker may keep up to $300 of broker funds in the account to cover account opening and maintenance costs (Idaho Code 54-2042).

questions.question_text: Under what limited circumstance may a broker keep personal funds in the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: To cover account costs, capped at $300
distractors: To earn interest on the combined balance
There is never a valid reason
questions.explanation: Idaho Code 54-2042 lets a broker keep no more than $300 of broker or firm funds in the trust account to pay for opening or maintaining the account, such as bank service charges. Anything beyond that is commingling.

questions.question_text: If a buyer and seller cannot agree on earnest money distribution, what should the broker avoid doing?
questions.correct_answer: Releasing funds to either party without authorization
distractors: Contacting both parties about the dispute
Notifying their designated broker of the dispute
questions.explanation: When earnest money is disputed, the broker must not release funds to either party without proper authorization. Guideline 14 provides procedures for handling the dispute, which may include interpleading the funds with the court.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes commingling from conversion of trust funds?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling is mixing funds; conversion is spending them
distractors: Commingling is more serious than conversion
Conversion applies only to broker funds
questions.explanation: Commingling is mixing personal and trust funds in the same account. Conversion is actually using or spending trust funds for personal purposes. Conversion is generally considered more serious because the funds are actually taken.
